protocols.notes_updates: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) - Total range 3-15 | Record best motor response | GCS Eye Opening: 4=Opening spontaneously, 3=Opening to verbal command, 2=Opening to pressure, 1=None | GCS Verbal Response: 5=Coherent speech, 4=Confused disorientated, 3=Words only, 2=Sounds only, 1=None | GCS Motor Response: 6=Obeys commands, 5=Localises painful stimulus, 4=Withdraws from painful stimulus, 3=Flexion to painful stimulus, 2=Extension to painful stimulus, 1=None | GCS <13 on initial assessment OR <15 at 2 hours after injury indicates need for CT | CT Imaging Criteria - Absolute indications: GCS <13 on initial assessment OR <15 at 2 hours after injury | Suspected open/depressed skull fracture/base of skull fracture/facial fractures | Post-traumatic seizure | Two or more episodes of vomiting since head injury | Intubated | Focal neurological deficit | Anticoagulant patients (Warfarin/DOAC): Perform non-contrast CT Brain and C-spine | Relative indications (if LOC or amnesia): Risk factors - Age >65, Bleeding/clotting disorder, Dangerous mechanism, >30min retrograde amnesia | If risk factors present: Perform CT Brain and C-spine | If absent: No imaging required | Dangerous mechanism: Pedestrian/cyclist struck by vehicle, Ejection from vehicle, Fall from height >1m or 5 stairs | Base of skull fracture signs: Raccoon eyes (periorbital ecchymosis), CSF otorrhoea/rhinorrhoea, Battle sign (bruising over mastoid process), Haemotympanum | Neuroprotective measures: Head up 30-45 degrees | MAP >90mmHg unless ICH then MAP >75-80mmHg | Normocarbia PCO2 34-40mmHg (lower end of normal) | Normoxia PaO2 60-100mmHg, O2 sats target >95% | Normothermia (Temp <37.5C) | Normoglycaemia (Blood glucose target 5-10 mmol/l) | Correct electrolyte abnormalities | Monitor for seizures - Phenytoin 20mg/kg IV max rate 50mg/min loading dose in traumatic ICH | Sedation and analgesia to avoid coughing/gagging (raises ICP) | Consider Fentanyl 1-3 mcg/kg over 60 seconds, 3 minutes prior to intubation | Avoid: Neck pressure (C-spine collars, tight ETT tape, CVP in IJV) | Excessive PEEP and elevated airway pressures | Steroids in traumatic ICH (no role) | Features of raised ICP - Early signs: Headache, vomiting, altered LOC, Visual alterations, abducens nerve palsies | Late signs: Cushings triad (Hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respiration), Unilateral dilated/non-reactive pupil with decreased GCS, Fixed constricted pupils | POCUS: Optic nerve sheath diameter >5mm | Discharge criteria: Normal conscious state GCS 15/15 | Patient no longer intoxicated | Responsible person available | Head Injury Discharge Advice Sheet provided | Discharge advice - Return if: Unconsciousness, Drowsiness >1 hour, Problems understanding/speaking/confusion, Loss balance/problems walking, Weakness arms/legs, Eyesight problems, Severe headache, Vomiting, Seizures, Bleeding from ears | Expected minor symptoms: Mild headache, nausea, dizziness, irritability, concentration problems, memory problems, tiredness, lack of appetite, sleep problems - should resolve within 2 weeks | Advice: Do not stay home alone first 24 hours | Stay within reach of telephone | Plenty rest, avoid stress | No alcohol or drugs | No sleeping pills/sedatives/tranquilisers unless prescribed | No contact sport for 3 weeks without doctor approval | No driving or machinery unless completely recovered | Admission/Referral: Neurosurgery registrar HJH during working hours; After 16:00 call CMJAH registrar | Normal hours - HJH under Neurosurgery; After hours - HJH under General Surgery (Neurosurgery take-over next morning) | Admission criteria: Surgical intervention required | Abnormal CT (neuro observation/IV antibiotics/O2) | Normal CT but not meeting discharge criteria | Admission required for other indications

diagnostic_criteria.ct_absolute_indications: GCS <13 initial OR <15 at 2hrs | Open/depressed skull fracture | Post-traumatic seizure | >2 vomiting episodes | Intubated | Focal neurological deficit
diagnostic_criteria.ct_relative_risk_factors: Age >65 | Bleeding/clotting disorder | Dangerous mechanism | >30min retrograde amnesia
diagnostic_criteria.dangerous_mechanism: Pedestrian/cyclist struck by vehicle | Ejection from vehicle | Fall >1m or 5 stairs
diagnostic_criteria.base_of_skull_fracture_signs: Raccoon eyes | CSF otorrhoea/rhinorrhoea | Battle sign | Haemotympanum

clinical_features.early_signs_racied_icp: Headache, vomiting, altered LOC, visual alterations, abducens nerve palsies
clinical_features.late_signs_raised_icp: Cushings triad (HTN, bradycardia, irregular respiration), unilateral dilated pupil, fixed constricted pupils

drugs.notes: 3 minutes prior to intubation, to blunt sympathetic response

management_steps.details: Eye opening, verbal, motor response. Record best motor response. GCS 3-15.
management_steps.caution: GCS <13 initially or <15 at 2hrs needs CT

management_steps.details: Check absolute and relative indications for CT Brain and C-spine
Head up 30-45deg, MAP >90 (or >75-80 if ICH), Normocarbia PCO2 34-40, Normoxia PaO2 60-100, Temp <37.5C, Glucose 5-10 mmol/l
management_steps.caution: Avoid neck pressure, excessive PEEP, steroids

management_steps.details: Early and late signs. POCUS optic nerve sheath diameter >5mm
GCS 15, not intoxicated, responsible person available = discharge with advice sheet. Otherwise admit.

protocols.monitoring: GCS monitoring | MAP >90mmHg (or >75-80 if ICH) | PCO2 34-40mmHg | PaO2 60-100mmHg O2 sats >95% | Temp <37.5C | Blood glucose 5-10 mmol/l | Optic nerve sheath diameter on POCUS | Seizure monitoring
warnings: GCS <13 on initial assessment or <15 at 2 hours after injury = CT needed
Avoid steroids in traumatic ICH - no role
Avoid neck pressure from C-spine collars, tight ETT tape, CVP in IJV
Avoid excessive PEEP and elevated airway pressures
Rabies immunoglobulin is CRITICAL in head/neck/face wounds due to short incubation period
protocols.disposition: Discharge: GCS 15/15, not intoxicated, responsible person available, discharge advice sheet given | Admission: Surgical intervention needed, abnormal CT, normal CT but not meeting discharge criteria, other indications | Neurosurgery registrar HJH working hours; CMJAH after 16:00

protocols.notes_updates: EFAST (Extended Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) | 2 accredited EFASTs required | EFAST cannot exclude retroperitoneal or hollow viscus injuries | EFAST can ONLY confirm: free fluid in abdomen, haemo-pneumothorax, pericardial fluid | Less sensitive/specific in pregnancy and paediatrics | RUSH protocol (Rapid Ultrasound for Shock and Hypotension) referenced as policy annexure | Standard RUSH views include: Heart (pericardial/subxiphoid), IVC, Lung (sliding/sign), Morrisons pouch/hepatorenal, Splenorenal, Suprapubic/pelvic, Paracolic gutters | Detailed RUSH protocol not available in extracted text

diagnostic_criteria.efast_can_confirm: Free fluid in abdomen | Haemo-pneumothorax | Pericardial fluid
diagnostic_criteria.efast_limitations: Less sensitive in pregnancy | Less sensitive in paediatrics
diagnostic_criteria.rush_views: Heart (pericardial/subxiphoid) | IVC | Lung (sliding/sign) | Morrisons pouch/hepatorenal | Splenorenal | Suprapubic/pelvic | Paracolic gutters

management_steps.details: 2 accredited EFASTs required. Assess for free fluid in abdomen, haemo/pneumothorax, pericardial fluid.
management_steps.caution: Cannot exclude retroperitoneal or hollow viscus injuries

management_steps.details: Assess heart (pericardial/subxiphoid), IVC, lung sliding, Morrisons pouch, splenorenal, suprapubic, paracolic gutters
management_steps.caution: RUSH protocol is detailed annexure - refer to full policy

protocols.monitoring: Serial EFASTs as clinically indicated | Correlation with clinical picture and other imaging
warnings: EFAST cannot exclude retroperitoneal or hollow viscus injuries
EFAST is less sensitive/specific in pregnancy and paediatrics
2 accredited EFASTs are required
Positive EFAST for free fluid only confirms presence, not source
protocols.disposition: EFAST findings guide further imaging (CT pan-scan) and surgical consultation as clinically indicated

{'low_risk': 'Non-specific pain < 6 weeks with no red flags, monoradiculopathy/sciatica - DC with simple analgesics and physio/spine OPD follow up', 'high_risk': 'Manage underlying cause, admit to appropriate service (orthopaedics/neurosurgery/ICU) if indicated'}
protocols.notes_updates: Low-risk: Non-specific pain < 6 weeks with no red flags, monoradiculopathy/sciatica - discharge with simple analgesics and physio/spine OPD follow up. High-risk: Manage underlying cause, admit to appropriate service (orthopaedics/neurosurgery/ICU) if indicated.

INDICATIONS FOR LAPAROTOMY: Evisceration | Haemodynamic instability | Peritonitis | Absent bowel sounds | Free air under diaphragm | Laparotomy required regardless of haemodynamic status | If stable with no peritonitis, CT abdomen with contrast | If unstable or peritonitis, immediate laparotomy | Gunshot wounds to abdomen: Laparotomy required | Do NOT remove impaled objects in ED - remove in theatre | Tetanus prophylaxis | Broad-spectrum antibiotics | Massive transfusion protocol if required

diagnostic_criteria.indications_for_laparotomy: Evisceration | Haemodynamic instability | Peritonitis | Absent bowel sounds | Free air under diaphragm
diagnostic_criteria.impaled_objects: Do NOT remove in ED - remove in theatre

drugs.notes: Give if tetanus status not up to date

management_steps.details: ABC approach. 2 large bore IV lines. Crossmatch. Massive transfusion if required.
Evisceration, peritonitis, absent bowel sounds, free air under diaphragm = laparotomy
management_steps.caution: Do NOT remove impaled objects in ED

management_steps.details: CT abdomen with contrast if stable and no peritonitis
management_steps.caution: Unstable patients need theatre, not CT

management_steps.details: Laparotomy for gunshot wounds, evisceration, haemodynamic instability, or peritonitis

protocols.monitoring: Haemodynamic monitoring | Peritonitis signs | Bowel sounds | Serial examinations
warnings: Do NOT remove impaled objects in ED - remove in theatre
Gunshot wounds to abdomen require laparotomy
Unstable patients need theatre, not CT
protocols.disposition: Laparotomy for: evisceration, haemodynamic instability, peritonitis, absent bowel sounds, free air, gunshot wounds | CT if stable with no peritonitis

protocols.notes_updates: BURN ASSESSMENT: Determine depth (1st, 2nd, 3rd degree) and extent (%TBSA) | Rule of 9s: Head 9%, Each arm 9%, Each leg 18%, Front torso 18%, Back torso 18%, Perineum 1% | For children: use Lund-Browder chart | Palm method: patient's palm (including fingers) = 1% TBSA | INHALATION INJURY: Suspect with: Burns in enclosed space, Facial burns, Singed nasal hairs, Soot in mouth/sputum, Hoarse voice, Stridor, Dyspnoea, Carboxyhaemoglobin elevated | MANAGEMENT: Airway first - early intubation if inhalation injury suspected (see difficult airway protocol) | 100% oxygen | CO poisoning: continue 100% O2 until carboxyhaemoglobin <5% | Fluid resuscitation: Parkland formula - 4ml/kg x %TBSA | Give half in first 8 hours, half in next 16 hours | Use Ringers Lactate | Weigh patient if possible | Catheterise and monitor urine output | Aim urine output 0.5-1ml/kg/hr adults, 1-2ml/kg/hr children | Escharotomies for circumferential full-thickness burns compromising circulation or ventilation | Tetanus prophylaxis | Analgesia | Hypothermia prevention | Avoid hypotonic fluids | Criteria for referral to burns centre: Partial thickness >10% TBSA, Full thickness burns, Burns involving face/hands/feet/genitalia/perineum/major joints, Electrical/chemical burns, Inhalation injury, Circumferential burns, Special populations (extremes of age, comorbidities)

diagnostic_criteria.rule_of_9s: Head 9%, Each arm 9%, Each leg 18%, Front torso 18%, Back torso 18%, Perineum 1%
diagnostic_criteria.inhalation_injury_signs: Burns in enclosed space, Facial burns, Singed nasal hairs, Soot in mouth/sputum, Hoarse voice, Stridor, Dyspnoea, Elevated carboxyhaemoglobin
diagnostic_criteria.parkland_formula: 4ml/kg x %TBSA. Half in first 8hrs, half in next 16hrs.

clinical_features.first_degree: Erythema, pain, no blisters. Heals in 3-6 days.
clinical_features.second_degree: Blisters, severe pain, weeping. Heals in 2-3 weeks.
clinical_features.third_degree: White/charred, leathery, painless (nerve destruction). Requires grafting.

drugs.notes: Half in first 8 hours, half in next 16 hours
Give if tetanus status not up to date

management_steps.details: Assess for inhalation injury. Early intubation if suspected. 100% oxygen.
Determine %TBSA using rule of 9s or Lund-Browder. Document depth (1st/2nd/3rd degree).
management_steps.caution: Use patient's palm = 1% TBSA for smaller areas

management_steps.details: Parkland formula: 4ml/kg x %TBSA. Half in first 8hrs, half in next 16hrs. Ringers Lactate.
management_steps.caution: Weigh patient. Catheterise for urine output monitoring.

management_steps.details: Clean with soap and water. Cover with sterile dressings. Escharotomies if circumferential full-thickness burns.
Tetanus prophylaxis, analgesia, urine output monitoring (0.5-1ml/kg/hr adults), consider referral to burns centre.

protocols.monitoring: Urine output: 0.5-1ml/kg/hr adults, 1-2ml/kg/hr children | Vital signs | Carboxyhaemoglobin levels | Fluid balance
warnings: Early intubation if inhalation injury suspected
Do not underestimate burn extent in children - use Lund-Browder
Escharotomies for circumferential burns compromising circulation or ventilation
Hypothermia prevention essential
Refer to burns centre for significant burns
protocols.disposition: Refer to burns centre for: Partial thickness >10% TBSA, Full thickness burns, Burns involving face/hands/feet/genitalia/perineum/major joints, Electrical/chemical burns, Inhalation injury, Circumferential burns, Extremes of age or comorbidities

protocols.notes_updates: GENERAL PRINCIPLES: Assess ABCs first | Neurovascular assessment before and after any manipulation | Document neurovascular status | Analgesia prior to manipulation | X-ray before and after reduction (unless open fracture or gross deformity requiring immediate reduction) | Open fractures: IV antibiotics, tetanus, photo wound, cover with saline-soaked sterile dressing, splint, urgent orthopaedic consult | Compartment syndrome: Pain out of proportion, Pain on passive stretch, Pallor, Paralysis, Paraesthesia, Pulselessness (LATE) | Compartment syndrome = EMERGENCY - fasciotomy within 6 hours | Dislocations: Reduce as soon as possible (easier before muscle spasm) | Post-reduction X-ray | Neurovascular check post-reduction | Immobilise | SLINGS: Collar and cuff: humeral fractures, shoulder dislocations (allows gravity to help alignment) | Broad arm sling: for support after reduction | High arm sling: clavicle fractures | Velpeau sling: humeral shaft fractures | Sager traction: femoral shaft fractures (temporary splinting) | SPECIFIC FRACTURES: Colles: Dinner fork deformity, reduce under haematoma block/Bier's block | Smith's: Garden spade deformity | Scaphoid: Tenderness in anatomical snuffbox, thumb spica cast, MRI if X-ray negative | NOF (Neck of Femur): Shortened, externally rotated leg, X-ray AP and lateral pelvis, DHS vs hemiarthroplasty | Intertrochanteric: DHS fixation | Subtrochanteric: Intramedullary nail | Femoral shaft: Significant blood loss (up to 1L), Sager traction, intramedullary nail | Tibial shaft: High risk compartment syndrome, check neurovascular status frequently | Ankle: Weber classification (A, B, C), check syndesmosis | Open fractures: Urgent orthopaedic consult, antibiotics, tetanus, debridement in theatre within 6 hours | Traction splints for femoral fractures | Back slab for most limb fractures in ED | Elevation for all limb fractures

diagnostic_criteria.compartment_syndrome_6_ps: Pain out of proportion, Pain on passive stretch, Pallor, Paralysis, Paraesthesia, Pulselessness (LATE)
diagnostic_criteria.open_fracture: Fracture with overlying wound - urgent orthopaedic consult, antibiotics, tetanus, debridement within 6hrs
diagnostic_criteria.dislocation_urgency: Reduce as soon as possible before muscle spasm sets in

drugs.notes: Give if tetanus status not up to date

management_steps.details: ABCs first. Assess and document neurovascular status distal to injury before any manipulation.
Provide analgesia. X-ray before reduction unless open fracture or gross deformity.
Reduce dislocations ASAP. Reduce fractures as indicated. Splint after reduction.
management_steps.caution: Neurovascular check before and after reduction

management_steps.details: Post-reduction X-ray. Neurovascular check. Immobilise. Elevate limb.
Open fractures: IV antibiotics, tetanus, photo, saline dressing, splint, urgent ortho. Compartment syndrome: Emergency fasciotomy within 6 hours.

protocols.monitoring: Neurovascular checks before and after reduction | Compartment syndrome signs | Pain assessment | Post-reduction X-rays
warnings: Compartment syndrome is an emergency - fasciotomy within 6 hours
Open fractures need urgent orthopaedic consult and debridement within 6 hours
Always document neurovascular status before and after manipulation
Reduce dislocations ASAP before muscle spasm sets in
Check scaphoid with MRI if X-ray negative but clinical suspicion high
protocols.disposition: Open fractures: Urgent orthopaedic consult, debridement in theatre within 6 hours | NOF fractures: Urgent orthopaedic consult | Dislocations: Reduce in ED, post-reduction X-ray, orthopaedic follow-up | Complex fractures: Orthopaedic consult
